common_name: Tangerine Beauty Crossvine (Native Apricot-Orange Tubular Flower Spring Vine)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Tangerine Beauty is one of the most cold-hardy and adaptable native flowering vines for Gulf South gardens, performing equally well in full sun and partial shade — one of few vines with significant shade tolerance.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Tangerine Beauty is a selected cultivar of crossvine (Bignonia capreolata), a native southeastern vine notable for its warm apricot-orange to copper-orange flower color — a softer, warmer tone than the typical red-and-yellow of the straight species. The 2-inch, tubular, trumpet-shaped flowers are produced in dense clusters along the vine in March–May, creating a spectacular spring display of orange-toned blooms that attract hummingbirds with exceptional consistency — the tubular flower form and warm color combination is highly attractive to ruby-throated hummingbirds during their spring migration through the Gulf South. Crossvine clings to surfaces with adhesive tendrils and disk-like holdfasts similar to Boston ivy — it can climb masonry, wood, and bark without a separate trellis. The semi-evergreen, compound leaves (two leaflets flanking a tendril) turn deep burgundy-red in winter cold, providing attractive winter foliage color before the spring bloom. Unlike many vines, Tangerine Beauty tolerates considerable shade — growing well on north-facing fences and under tree canopy where most flowering vines fail. The native origin makes it valuable for wildlife gardens: hummingbirds in spring, and specialist native bee species that visit the flowers. The vine is manageable in typical garden settings, growing to 10–20 feet rather than the 50+ feet of some other vines.

common_name: Jambalaya Okra (Compact Dwarf Productive Okra for Small Gardens)

planting.note: Direct sow 1 inch deep after the last frost when soil is warm (70°F+) in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Jambalaya's compact size (36–42 inches vs 6–8 feet for standard okra) makes it practical for raised beds and container growing — even a 5-gallon container supports one plant.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after the last frost in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Jambalaya is a compact, dwarf okra hybrid producing the full flavor and productivity of standard okra on a plant roughly half the height of traditional okra varieties — reaching only 3–4 feet versus the 6–8 feet of older varieties like Clemson Spineless. The compact size makes Jambalaya practical for raised beds, containers (5-gallon minimum), and gardens where standard-height okra would create shade problems or require staking. Despite the reduced height, fruit production per plant is excellent — the compact branching structure holds pods within easy reach without the ladder-or-bending situation that harvesting from full-height okra plants requires. The pods are standard green okra with the classic flavor, tender at 3–4 inches and maturing at the typical rapid okra pace (daily checking required at peak production). Jambalaya's compact habit also makes it attractive as a temporary summer ornamental — the large, lightly lobed leaves, yellow hibiscus-like flowers with red centers, and developing pods create a tropical visual effect that is ornamental as well as productive. In zone 9, Jambalaya thrives through the hottest Gulf South summer months when most other vegetables are stressed — okra is one of the few crops that genuinely performs at its best during July and August heat.

common_name: Calico Crowder Pea (Heirloom Multi-Color Seed Crowder Southern Pea)

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost when soil has warmed above 65°F in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Calico Crowder is a vining type — provide a trellis or fence for support, or allow plants to sprawl on the ground in traditional field-pea planting style.

directions: Direct sow after the last frost when soil is warm in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Calico Crowder is an heirloom Southern pea producing the characteristic multi-colored seeds that give it its calico name — tan, brown, black, and cream mottled seeds with a distinctive pattern that creates a beautiful, rustic visual when dried and stored, distinguishing this traditional variety from the uniform-colored modern selections. Crowder peas are named for their growing habit: the seeds are so tightly packed in the pod that they are 'crowded' and develop flattened sides where they press against each other — this crowding is a defining type characteristic that distinguishes them from black-eyed peas and cream peas. The crowder texture — denser, starchier, and more potato-like than cream peas — produces a hearty, substantial broth when slow-cooked, which is the traditional cooking method for old-field varieties like Calico. As a vining-type crowder, Calico produces well on a trellis or fence and can be managed in a smaller space than sprawling bush types. The heirloom seeds can be saved year after year from the dried pods. In zone 9, Calico Crowder can be planted from April through August for multiple successive crops through the long warm season — Southern peas are uniquely suited to Gulf South heat that stops most other legumes.

common_name: Texas Cream 8 Pea (Compact High-Yield Cream Pea for Gulf South Gardens)

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost when soil has warmed above 65°F in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Texas Cream 8 is a semi-determinate, compact bush type that can be grown without staking — the self-supporting, upright plants concentrate pod set for a defined harvest window.

directions: Direct sow after the last frost when soil is warm in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Texas Cream 8 is a compact, high-yielding cream southern pea selected through the Texas Agricultural Experiment Station breeding program — producing uniform, cream-colored seeds in short, upright plants that are self-supporting and well-suited to intensive raised-bed production. The 'Cream 8' number designation indicates its classification in the cream-type southern pea category, where seeds are creamy-white to tan without the eye coloring of black-eyed peas or the mottled colors of crowder types. Cream peas have a mild, sweet, delicate flavor considered more refined than the earthier crowder types — they are prized for their tender texture when fresh-shelled and their clean, creamy appearance in finished dishes. Texas Cream 8 has performed consistently well in Gulf Coast trials for productivity, uniformity, and disease resistance. The compact, semi-determinate bush habit allows closer spacing (3–4 inches in rows) than standard sprawling cream peas, increasing productivity per garden square foot. The concentrated pod set makes Texas Cream 8 practical for batch shelling and freezing — the defined harvest window means a larger quantity of pods reaches maturity simultaneously, enabling more efficient processing than the trickle harvest of indeterminate types.

common_name: Red Noodle Yard-Long Bean (Striking Deep-Red Extra-Long Asian Pole Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost when soil has warmed above 70°F in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; provide a trellis or fence at least 5 feet tall — Red Noodle vines grow vigorously to 10+ feet and require substantial support for the trailing bean clusters.

directions: Direct sow after the last frost when soil is warm in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Red Noodle is a striking red-podded yard-long bean (Vigna unguiculata subsp. sesquipedalis) producing deep burgundy-red to dark red pods 18–24 inches long — the dramatic red color and extraordinary length combine to make this one of the most visually spectacular edible climbing plants available for Gulf South summer gardens. Yard-long beans (also called asparagus beans or Chinese long beans) are a tropical subspecies of cowpea adapted to hot, humid conditions that make them particularly suitable for Gulf South summer production when standard snap beans decline. The pods should be harvested at 12–18 inches for best tenderness and flavor — they become tough and fibrous when allowed to develop past 20 inches. Red Noodle maintains its red color when raw and in raw preparations but turns greenish-brown when cooked — similar to the color loss in Burgundy snap beans and Red Russian kale. The cooking texture is similar to standard yard-long beans: slightly firmer and less watery than snap beans, holding texture well in stir-fries and sautés. The vines grow vigorously to 8–12 feet in Gulf South heat and require substantial trellis support (minimum 5–6 foot fence or trellis height) — the trailing red bean clusters hanging from overhead structures create a dramatic ornamental and productive display simultaneously.
